messages.content: You validate HVAC manual parameter extractions. Reply only with strict JSON.

ACCEPT as parameter_spec when the item is a configurable setpoint, limit, mode selection, enable/disable selection, default setting, adjustable range, or an operator-visible active/arbitrated setpoint shown on a control panel table. REJECT when the item is a command/action, UI navigation description, product marketing claim, internal algorithm timing, component/module name, relay output label, analog/digital input or output label, signal name, terminal mapping, wiring point, fault code, or design-condition performance spec. Examples to reject as category errors: Relay Output Status, 1A17 Analog Input/Output Module, Digital Switch Input Module, External Base Loading Setpoint input signal label.

This fits: it's a default setting (default 10 min) with adjustable range (0-120 min). The fact that it's a filter time might make it "internal algorithm timing". But many configurable parameters are internal algorithm parameters. The distinction is whether it's a parameter that an operator can see and adjust on a control panel. Let's read carefully: "REJECT when the item is a command/action, UI navigation description, product marketing claim, internal algorithm timing, component/module name, relay output label, analog/digital input or output label, signal name, terminal mapping, wiring point, fault code, or design-condition performance spec." But if it's configurable and shown on a control panel table, it might still be a parameter_spec. The instruction says: "ACCEPT as parameter_spec when the item is a configurable setpoint, limit, mode selection, enable/disable selection, default setting, adjustable range, or an operator-visible active/arbitrated setpoint shown on a control panel table." It doesn't explicitly exclude internal algorithm timing if it's configurable. But it says "internal algorithm timing" as a rejection reason. So there's a conflict. Which takes precedence? The phrase "internal algorithm timing" might refer to timings that are not meant to be configured by the operator but are fixed in the firmware, or maybe timings that are part of the internal logic but not exposed as a parameter. However, the evidence says it can be set, so it is exposed. The instruction might be using "internal algorithm timing" to mean times that are hardcoded and not configurable. But it's ambiguous. I think it should be accepted. But let's see if there's any hint: the source page is 51. Possibly from a manual. It's described with a default and range, so it's clearly a parameter. The instruction says "REJECT when the item is ... internal algorithm timing". If we interpret that literally, this is an algorithm timing. But I think the intention is to reject descriptions of internal timings that are not configurable (like "the PID loop updates every 100 ms") rather than configurable timers. The examples given for rejection are things that are not parameters: Relay Output Status (a status, not a setting), module names, input signal labels. So "internal algorithm timing" likely means a fixed timing that is part of the algorithm design, not a settable parameter. But a settable filter time is a parameter. So I'll accept it. ACCEPT as parameter_spec when the item is a configurable setpoint, limit, mode selection, enable/disable selection, default setting, adjustable range, or an operator-visible active/arbitrated setpoint shown on a control panel table. Mode selections count even without a numeric value when the manual shows operator-selectable options, such as Chilled Water Reset Return / Constant Return / Outdoor / None.

REJECT when the item is a command/action, UI navigation description, product marketing claim, internal algorithm timing, component/module name, relay output label, analog/digital input or output label, signal name, terminal mapping, wiring point, fault code, or design-condition performance spec. Examples to reject as category errors: Relay Output Status, 1A17 Analog Input/Output Module, Digital Switch Input Module, External Base Loading Setpoint input signal label.

unit: %

This appears to be a parameter for minimum capacity limit, default 0%. It seems like a setpoint or limit. It likely is a configurable parameter that an operator can set. So it counts as a parameter_spec. 

Hence, return true with reason.

message.content: {"is_parameter_spec": false, "reason": "Manufacturer-set limit, not operator-configurable", "category_if_not": "other"}
message.reasoning_content:
We are asked to validate whether "冷凝器限制设定" (Condenser Limit Setting) is a configurable operational parameter. The evidence says: "冷凝器限制设定由厂家设置（HPC的93%）" which translates to "Condenser limit setting is set by the manufacturer (93% of HPC)". HPC likely stands for High Pressure Cutout or similar. This seems like a factory-set limit, not something an operator can configure. It's a default setting set by the manufacturer, but might still be configurable? The instruction says: ACCEPT when it is a configurable setpoint, limit, mode selection, etc. Here it says "由厂家设置" (set by manufacturer), implying it's not typically adjusted by the operator. However, the instruction also mentions "default setting" as acceptable. But it's a limit that is set at a fixed percentage (93% of HPC) by the manufacturer. Is this something shown on a control panel table? Possibly, but the evidence doesn't indicate it's operator-configurable. It might be a fixed factory setting. Given the strict criteria, "configurable operational parameter" means something the operator can adjust. The phrase "由厂家设置" suggests it's a factory preset, not an operator adjustment. So it likely should be rejected as "other" or maybe "performance_spec"? It's not a command, UI navigation, etc. It's more of a design-condition performance spec or internal limit. I'd categorize as "other" because it's a manufacturer-set limit, not a user-configurable parameter. So reject.

Thus: {"is_parameter_spec": false, "reason": "Manufacturer-set limit, not operator-configurable", "category_if_not": "other"}

ACCEPT criteria: configurable setpoint, limit, mode selection, enable/disable, default setting, adjustable range, operator-visible active/arbitrated setpoint. REJECT criteria: command/action, UI navigation, marketing claim, internal algorithm timing, component/module name, relay output label, I/O label, signal name, terminal mapping, wiring point, fault code, design-condition performance spec.

The parameter_name translates to "Maximum pressure difference set value". Evidence: "Maximum pressure difference standard setting is 30 psi". It has unit psid (psi differential). It seems like a configurable setpoint (maximum differential pressure). Evidence says "standard设为30 psi", which might indicate a default, and "最大压差设定值" suggests it's a setpoint that can be adjusted. It is likely a configurable operational parameter. It is not a command, not UI navigation, not marketing, not internal algorithm timing, not a component/module name, not a relay output or I/O label, not a signal name, not terminal mapping, not fault code. It appears to be a setpoint for a differential pressure limit. So ACCEPT.

Thus, is_parameter_spec: true, reason: "Configurable setpoint for maximum differential pressure limit." category_if_not null should be null.
